data: C. Wessel, Inscriptiones Graecae Christianae Veteres Occidentis, Inscriptiones Christianae Italiae (Bari: Edipuglia, 1989)

The text appears to observes the shape of the stone, with a fixed left margin and the lines fitting to the line of breakage on the right, and a staurogram centrally filling the space below. The letters retain traces of red paint throughout.

Slightly irregular but neatly carved plain letters without serifs. Alpha has straight bar; Epsilon is rectilinear, with mostly equal length bars; Theta is large and voice, with a full width horizontal bar; Kappa generally has short arms; Lamda has a slight extension at the top; Mu (in ligature) has vertical virst and last, with diagonals extending only half-way down;' rho has small head, closed; sigma is lunate but rectilinear; omega is lunate. Interpuncts are used around numerals.
Photo Museo Arch. Reg. P. Orsi (2012), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Orsi, 2018, I Taccuini. I. Riproduzione anastatica e trascrizione dei Taccuini 1-4
Text encoding: Valentina Mignosa, Jonathan Prag
410 CE (dated to the year following the consulship of Honorius and Theodosius (409))
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana
Agnello, 1953, Silloge di iscrizioni paleocristiane della Sicilia
Ferrua, 1982-1983, Le iscrizioni datate della Sicilia paleocristiana
Manganaro, 1993, Greco nei pagi e latino nelle città della Sicilia romana tra I e VI sec. d.C.
Orsi, 1893, Siracusa. Esplorazioni nelle catacombe di S. Giovanni ed in quelle della vigna Cassia
Wessel, 1989, Inscriptiones Graecae Christianae Veteres Occidentis
Text on two lines, with final letter of line 1 squeezed in small before the broken edge of the stone; two birds and christian monograms fill the space below.

Moderately neat and regular letters, cut with a broad groove rather than v-cut, without scerifs, but with extensions to tops of some letters (alpha, delta). alpha has broken bar; epsilon is lunate; kappa has full length arms; omicron is full size; rho has small almost closed eye; sigma is lunate but rectilinear; phi is circular with extended vertical; omega (in symbols below) is lunate.
Carini, 1872, Annotazioni sul sarcofago rinvenuto in Siracusa
Funerary inscription for Eirene and Theodoulos
hederae at the four corners, and two christograms to the right of the final line in a circle, with a cross between them and below.
Crispi, 1823, Iscrizioni sepolcrali greche appartenenti all'epoca cristiana, interpetrate ed illustrate
Ferrua, 1989, Note e giunte alle iscrizioni cristiane antiche della Sicilia
Strazzulla, 1897, Museum epigraphicum: seu inscriptionum Christianarum quae in Syracusanis catacumbis repertae sunt corpusculum
Muscolino, 2011-2012, Epigrafi funerarie greche e latine di Taormina
funerary inscription for eirene and theodoulos
over five lines, consistent left margin.
traces of red paint noted in the inventory

The text maintains a regular left margin, ignoring the irregularity of the form of the stone, and filling the space to the right; however, the cutter ran out of space at the end of the stone, and inserted the remaining letters of the word Δεκεμβρίῳ and the concluding staurogram, firstly at the end of line 2 (letter β) and then, under a 'paragraphos' line to the left of the main text of line 2, level with lines 3 (letters ριω) and 4 (staurogram)

plainly cut, relatively regular letters, in a curved groove rather than v-cut, without serifs. Alpha has an oblique crossbar, sloping down to left, and a horizontal stroke to left off the top of the letter; beta has closed eyes, the upper is smaller; epsilon is lunate (except for the first in line 5 which is rectilinear); zeta has regular Z form; kappa has near full-legnth arms; mu has cursive form; omicron is full size; rho has closed eye; sigma is lunate; omega is lunate.
Photo Museo Arch. Reg. P. Orsi (2015), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
Feissel, 1982, Remarques de toponymie syrienne d'après des inscriptions grecques chrétiennes trouvées hors de Syrie
Ahlquist, 1995, Pitture e Mosaici nei cimiteri paleo-cristiani di Siracusa. Corpus Iconographicum
Ferrua, 1940, Nuovi studi nelle catacombe di Siracusa
Five lines of Greek letters following the left margin of the stone.
Somewhat irregular lunate letters, tending strongly to cursive, especially the eta, alpha, mu and xsi
later Roman, but not certain whether Christian or Pagan (Ferrua)
Ferrua, 1941, Epigrafica sicula pagana e cristiana
Text encoding: James Hua, Jonathan Prag

Seven lines of Greek letters, with traces of red paint. Lines 1-3 are more formally / monumentally carved, from line four onwards the carving is lighter and less carefully laid out, rising to the right.

Simple, thinly incised letters without serfis or terminations. The letters become increasingly ireregular and variable in the final lines. Alpha has striaght bar but also broken and oblique bar; beta has closed roughly equal eyes; Epsilon is quadrate with equal length bars; Zeta is Z in form but irregular; theta is ovoid with full straight bar; kappa has shorter arms; omicron mostly slightly smaller than other letters; sigma is lunate but square in form, lower bar slanting downwards, except for the irregular curved instance end of line 2; omega is in the form of a w, but formed entirely of straight lines and right angles. interlineation is highly irregular throughout.
Photo J. Prag (2018), Aut. Assessorato Beni Culturali Regione Siciliana n.10681 del 06/05/2014
L'année épigraphique: revue des publications épigraphiques relatives a l'antiquité romaine., 1888-
Clarke, 2014, Il fascino della Cava. La storia dei Viaggiatori Inglesi a Cava Ispica
Rizzone, 2016, Una sinagoga a Cava Ispica
Bergk, 1843, Inschriften aus den Sicilischen Cyclopenhoehlen
The material from cava d'Ispica is bounded by two dated texts, of 398 CE and 468 CE, and these provide approximate lower and upper limits to the likely range (so Paci)
Castelli, 1784, Siciliae et objacentium insularum veterum inscriptionum nova collectio prolegomenis et notis illustrata, et iterum cum emendationibus, & auctariis evulgata
Monument with cornice bearing the name Chrysis
The text in the fourth panel runs from bottom to top and appears to be written retrograde.
Neat regular letters, with quadrate lunate sigma.
monument with cornice bearing the name chrysis

Six lines, of which lines 2-6 are indented by one letter compared to line 1, with a consistent left margin. The final line is shorter with the numeral larger and roughly centred. Mommsen suggested that the age formula in lines 5-6 was a later addition, seemingly for its slightly greater irregularity.
Lunate epsilon and sigma, cursive omega, with terminations rather than serifs; use of ligatures. Delta has extended top stroke. Some curvature of longer strokes and arms.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag, 2018-09-14
Kaibel, 1878, Epigrammata graeca ex lapidibus conlecta
Manganaro, 1994, Iscrizioni, epigrafi ed epigrammi in Greco della Sicilia orientale di epoca romana
Text encoding: Andoni Llamazares, Jonathan Prag
Irregular letters: lunate Ε and Σ, cursive Μ and Ω. Α, Δ and Λ with accentuated upper serifs. Chi-Rho symbol in the middle of l. 6.
Castelli, 1769, Siciliae et objacentium insularum veterum inscriptionum nova collectio
Ferrara, 1829, Storia di Catania sino alla fine del secolo XVIII
Funerary inscription for Adrianos and Deuteria
Gualtherus, 1624 [1625], Siciliæ obiacentium insular. et Bruttiorum antiquæ tabulæ, cum animadversionib
funerary inscription for adrianos and deuteria
The remains of one line of Greek letters, preceded by a plain cross (which may be a later addition), are preserved on the moulding at the base of the relief.

Slightly irregularly disposed letters, with a hedera between the first two words. In the text preserved by Gualtherus lunate epsilon, sigma and cursive omega are recorded, and interpuncts before the numerals.
Later 2nd century CE or first half of 3rd century CE
Text encoding: Marta Fogagnolo, Jonathan Prag
Epitaph for Aurelios Restoutos and Zosime
Irregular letters: lunate Ε and Σ; cursive Μ and Ω; Α and Λ with extended serifs.
4th century CE or first half of 5th century CE
epitaph for aurelios restoutos and zosime
Chi-Rho symbol at l. 1. Lunate Ε and Σ; cursive Ξ and Ω; A with slanted bar; Υ is similar to the Latin V.
Irregular letters, Α and Δ with serifs; quadrate Ε; nearly rhomboid O at l. 2.
Ferrua, 1938, Osservazioni sulle iscrizioni cristiane catanesi
Califano, 1997, Su un'iscrizione funeraria cristiana di Catania
Funerary inscription for Kallistos and Eukarpia
Paint in the lettters, but probably modern, and at least one letter (end of line 1) is omitted in the painting, as is sigma in ligature in line 2.
rhomboid omicron and omega; Tau is consistently tall than other letters.
funerary inscription for kallistos and eukarpia
Quadrate Ε, square lunate Σ, cursive Ω. Two Christograms with the letters A and Σ at start and end of inscription and a dove holding a twig below.
Letters with serifs: Α with broken bar, quadrate Ε, square lunate Σ, rhomboid Θ, cursive Ω (Ω at l. 5 as a Μ upside down). Letters at ll. 1-2 are slightly larger than letters in the following lines.
Irregular letters: angular Ε, lunate Σ, cursive Ω. A with a slanted bar (except at l. 1 where the bar is straight), Υ with the form of the Latin V. Interpuncts divide numerals from words.
Angular Ε and Σ, cursive Ω, Α, Δ, Λ with extended serifs, Chi-Rho symbol at the end of the inscription.
Text encoding: Marta Fogagnolo, Alex Antoniou

Lunate Ε (oft confused with Σ) and Σ, cursive Μ and Ω, A with straight bar (l. 1) and broken bar (l. 5). A vertical stroke divides ἔτη from the numeral. Presence of guide-lines, a Christogram and a cross.
Alessi, 1833, III. Giuseppe Alessi al chiarissimo segretario dell'instituto archeologico, salute.
Orsi, 1896, Gli scavi a S. Giovanni a Siracusa
Regular letters with apices, lunate Ε and Σ, cursive Ω, A, Δ and Λ with extended serifs. Two Christograms at l. 2.
Orsi, 1876-, Notizie degli scavi di antichità
Manganaro, 1988, La Sicilia da Sesto Pompeo a Diocleziano
Thinly incised letters; line 5 is smaller than the rest.
Orsi describes the writing as very irregular semi-cursive, scratched in the first two lines, formless ('informi') in the others.
Rough and superficial letters ("rozze e superficiali lettre").
Described by Orsi as 'a buone lettere'.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 1.10.2019
Orsi 1893 records that the letters are faint on the worn surface.
Orsi, 1895, Nuove esplorazioni nelle catacombe di S. Giovanni nel 1894
Six densely ordered lines of neat Greek letters; staurogram to left of lines 3-4.
Neatly v-cut Greek letters with minimal serifs and some extended strokes. Alpha is tending towards the cursive form. Lunate epsilon and sigma, uncial omega; mu tending to cursive.
Dated to the 25 January 402 CE, by the consulship of Vincentius of 401 (omitting his colleague, Fravitus).

A with straight bar (l. 2) and slanting bar (l. 1, l. 5), squared Ε and Σ, ligature between Π and Ρ at l. 6. Double dot at l. 6.
A, Δ and Λ with serifs, A with broken bar, squared Ε and Σ (but cursive E at l. 4), Ρ at l. 4 with extended upright.
Text incised in two lines, across the left and right sides of the upper part of a large cross incised across the middle of the brick.
